coredata.dc:title: Effect of fluorescence color of carbon quantum dots on the optical properties of LaFe0.5Cu0.5O3 nanocomposite and quinoline photocatalytic degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Lu
Fan, Jian
Li, Yumei
Sun, Peng
Zhang, Lianke
coredata.dc:description: The environmentally friendly semiconductor photocatalyst has tremendous potential for resolving wastewater pollution issues. This study describes the successful fabrication of a novel (LaFe0.5Cu0.5O3) LFC0.5/CQDs nanocomposite using a simple cryogenic-calcination method. The LFC0.5/G-CQDs composite displayed the greatest light absorption range and photogenerated carrier separation ability based on their various optical characteristics and electron transfer capabilities. Moreover, its quinoline degradation kinetics were 14 times greater than those of pure LFC0.5. According to the findings of a thorough investigation, this excellent photocatalytic performance may be ascribed to the synergistic effects of the up-converted fluorescent properties of G-CQDs, tuned band structure, and effective charge separation caused by the formation of heterojunction. Based on the findings of the experimental quenching, it was clear that the LFC0.5/G-CQDs photocatalytic process was dominated by the radicals ∙OH and ∙O2 −. A potential Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism for quinoline degradation by LFC0.5/G-CQDs was elucidated using band structure analysis and EPR data. In addition, the composite photocatalyst LFC0.5/G-CQDs demonstrated excellent reusability over five cycles. In addition to developing highly efficient visible-light-driven photocatalysts for degrading Quinoline, this study suggests that implementing carbon quantum dots at the center of the main catalyst's luminescence will maximize light utilization and enhance photocatalytic performance.

head.ce:title: Effect of fluorescence color of carbon quantum dots on the optical properties of LaFe0.5Cu0.5O3 nanocomposite and quinoline photocatalytic degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Environment, Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology, Baotou 014010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy and Environment, Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology, Baotou 014010, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic photocatalytic mechanism for the LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites.

The environmentally friendly semiconductor photocatalyst has tremendous potential for resolving wastewater pollution issues. This study describes the successful fabrication of a novel (LaFe0.5Cu0.5O3) LFC0.5/CQDs nanocomposite using a simple cryogenic-calcination method. The LFC0.5/G-CQDs composite displayed the greatest light absorption range and photogenerated carrier separation ability based on their various optical characteristics and electron transfer capabilities. Moreover, its quinoline degradation kinetics were 14 times greater than those of pure LFC0.5. According to the findings of a thorough investigation, this excellent photocatalytic performance may be ascribed to the synergistic effects of the up-converted fluorescent properties of G-CQDs, tuned band structure, and effective charge separation caused by the formation of heterojunction. Based on the findings of the experimental quenching, it was clear that the LFC0.5/G-CQDs photocatalytic process was dominated by the radicals ∙OH and ∙O2 −. A potential Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism for quinoline degradation by LFC0.5/G-CQDs was elucidated using band structure analysis and EPR data. In addition, the composite photocatalyst LFC0.5/G-CQDs demonstrated excellent reusability over five cycles. In addition to developing highly efficient visible-light-driven photocatalysts for degrading Quinoline, this study suggests that implementing carbon quantum dots at the center of the main catalyst's luminescence will maximize light utilization and enhance photocatalytic performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen heterocyclic compounds, as typical organic pollutants, have recently been detected across ambient groundwater, pharmaceutical wastewater, and coking wastewater [1,2]. Once they are exposed to the environment, it has been reported that they show “triptane” toxicity in a variety of organisms [3]. Due to its high abundance, prolonged removal times, and trouble in degrading to non-toxic and non-hazardous substances like CO2 and H2O, quinoline is one of these heterocyclic nitrogen-containing organic matter that is very difficult to remove [4]. Due to its inability to undergo biodegradation, quinoline has been extensively detected in pharmaceutical and coking wastewater. The biochemical effluent of coking has a quinoline concentration of 10 mg/L or higher [5–7]. Several studies have shown that quinoline produces irreversible cell damage and, in some cases, cancer in humans [8,9]. Therefore, the development of efficient, ecologically friendly quinoline treatment methods is urgently needed (see Scheme 1).

Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been identified as one of the most effective technologies for the extensive remediation of organic pollutants such as quinolone [10–12]. Visible light-driven semiconductor photocatalysis is among the most promising of these technologies because of its high efficiency, potential solar energy utilization, and high mineralization rate [13–15]. Various photocatalysts, including TiO2, g-C3N4, and AgPO4, have been discovered to be effective for quinoline removal to date [16,17]. However, most photocatalysts have a wide band gap and can only utilize a small fraction of sunlight, affecting their overall photocatalytic performance [18]. As a narrow bandgap photocatalyst, lanthanum ferrate (LaFeO3) has attracted considerable interest due to its suitable band gap (2.10 eV), easy synthesis, and nontoxicity [19]. It has been established that partial doping of A-site elements (Fe, Sr, Pb, and Pd) in LaFeO3 species can increase oxygen vacancies. Contrarily, B-site doping (Ba, Cu, Ti) can decrease the energy band gap, thereby enhancing the utilization of visible light [20–22]. Doping Cu elements at the B-site in LaFeO3 to form Cu-Fe bimetallic oxides has been regarded as a highly promising semiconductor photocatalyst in recent years because of their simple synthesis, well-separated photogenerated carriers, and low toxicity [23,24]. This group's prior research demonstrated that doping Cu with molten salt increases its light absorption and reaction site, reduces its charge transfer resistance, and promotes active metal recycling. In the photocatalytic system, Cu+ reduced Fe3+ to Fe2+ because Cu2+/Cu+ had a lower redox potential than Fe3+/Fe2+. The superior redox cycling properties of Cu2+/Cu+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ are essential to the photocatalytic process [25]. Despite advances in synthetic methods, the photocatalytic activity of LaFe0.5Cu0.5O3 (LFC0.5) remains limited. This may be due to the rapid recombination of photogenerated e- and h+ that have been scattered or captured in various defect states, as well as the accumulation of catalyst particles in the liquid solution, resulting in agglomeration phenomena that directly impact the photocatalytic efficiency of the reaction. Therefore, it is of the utmost importance to modify these low-bandgap photocatalysts with carbon nanomaterials [26].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted significant interest in recent years due to their exceptional optical properties, electron storage properties, high water solubility, simple preparation, and low cost. Consequently, CQDs are regarded as “green” materials for large-scale applications [27–29]. Some researchers claim that CQDs can efficiently excite charge carriers by absorbing visible light at longer wavelengths and then emitting UV light at shorter wavelengths [30]. Due to the presence of several hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups on their surfaces, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl, CQDs have an enhanced surface state and a high water solubility, allowing them to bind with other materials [31]. Thus, CQDs have recently been employed to improve the optical absorption efficiency of semiconductor catalysts[32]. The modification of photocatalysts with CQDs broadens the light absorption range, thereby promoting photoexcited electron transfer at their interfaces and permitting stable charge separation [33]. This causes the formation of long-lasting cavities on the surface, which enhances photocatalytic activity [34,35]. CQDs and LFC0.5 can possibley improve photocatalytic activity by forming heterogeneous frameworks. However, little is known about the heterostructural mechanism of LFC0.5 /CQDs composites. To the best of our knowledge, no effort has ever been made to develop CQDs modified LFC0.5 composite catalysts to improve their photocatalytic activity. However, only one visible spectrum band is occupied by the light absorption range of CQDs [36]. Researchers have produced some preliminary findings in their research of CQDs with various luminescent colors. According to Zhu et al., CQDs with various emission patterns were able to selectively sense ions depending on the band structure and functional groups on the surface [37]. According to Sun et al., various luminescence colors for CQDs represent different fluorescence intensities and quantum yields, which is important for the fields of bioimaging and photocatalysis [38]. Multicolor fluorescent CQDs have very distinct characteristics in terms of fluorescence lifetime, light absorption range, and yield [39,40]. When combined with semiconductor photocatalysts, these factors can have a significant impact on the photocatalytic effect [41]. Therefore, it is necessary to produce CQDs with multicolor emission properties for the synthesis of LFC0.5 and to identify the most effective catalysts.

In this study, five different luminescent colors of fluorescent CQDs were prepared using the aqueous (solvent) thermal method and loaded onto the surface of LFC0.5 to create a novel LFC0.5/CQDs heterogeneous photocatalyst. The photocatalytic degradation performance of LFC0.5/CQDs was demonstrated using quinoline as the target pollutant. First, owing to their up-converted fluorescent properties, CQDs were able to excite the LFC0.5 by converting visible light to ultraviolet light. Second, the CQDs promoted the formation of long-lived holes and photoexcited electron transfer, which is advantageous for oxidation reactions. CQDs facilitated dispersion and homogenous compounding with LFC0.5 due to their high water solubility. Using characterization and radical production measurements, the relationship between light absorption range, energy band alignments, and charge transfer of the LFC0.5 modified by CQDs was extensively investigated. A potential photocatalytic decomposition mechanism of LFC0.5/CQDs for quinoline decomposition was also suggested.

Quinoline, Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O), Urea (CH4N2O), Ammonium citratetribasic (C6H17N3O7), Ethyl-alcohol (C2H5OH), N,N-Dimethylformamide (C3H7NO), Cupric oxide (CuO), Ferric sesquioxide (Fe2O3), Lanthanum oxide (La2O3), Sodium chloride (NaCl), and Potassium chloride (KCl) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of an analytical grade or higher and are utilized as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of multicolor fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Five CQDs with various luminescent colors were made by varying the reaction conditions, including the reaction duration, the ratio of carbon and nitrogen sources, the solvents used, and the hydrothermal temperatures. Different amounts of citric acid and urea (p-phenylenediamine) were dissolved in different solvents (water, ethanol, and DMF 20 mL) and homogenized in an ultrasonic cleaner for 30 min. The solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor and heated for a predetermined period (Table 1 ). The final CQDs solution was collected after it had cooled to room temperature naturally. It was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min in a high-speed centrifuge. The supernatant was then dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis tube for 48 h in the dark. The solution obtained was freeze-dried into a powder and designated X-CQDs (X = B, G, R, C, O).

An electronic balance was used to measure the required amounts of iron oxide, copper oxide, and lanthanum oxide. (molar ratio: 0.5:0.5:1). As the melting agent, a mixture of NaCl and KCl was made with a mass ratio of 1:1 between the two chemicals. Several drugs were then thoroughly ground until homogeneous and put in a crucible, where they were calcined at 800° for five hours with a 5 °C/min heating rate in a muffle furnace. To produce the LFC0.5 sample, the brown product was washed three times with deionized water and ethanol (0.22 μm filtration membrane), dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C, and then homogeneously ground.

A certain amount of LFC0.5 was dispersed in 30 mL of Ethanol for 20 min using ultrasonic treatment. The mixture was agitated magnetically for 10 min. Then, 3 %, 5 %, and 10 % of CQDs solid particles were added to the mixture, which was then heated in a water bath set to 60 °C until the solvent evaporated. The acquired precipitates were heated in an oven with hot air at 105 °C for 2 h. Finally, the LFC0.5/CQDs composite photocatalyst was obtained by calcining the mixture at 300 °C in a muffle furnace for 4 h.
Characterizations were discussed in the Text S1.

The original quinoline solution was diluted to a concentration of 20 mg/L to simulate a pollutant concentration. A quantity of the prepared catalyst was added and stored in a dark container to prevent light exposure. After 30 min, the dark adsorption reaction attained equilibrium between adsorption and desorption. To simulate sunlight in the photocatalytic degradation experiment, the 300-watt xenon lamp was activated at this time. The samples were collected at predetermined time intervals, filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane to remove catalyst particles from the solution, and then analyzed by HPLC. All experiments were conducted three times, with a 3 % experimental error on average. The pH values of solutions were adjusted to 3.00 ± 0.10, 5.00 ± 0.10, 7.00 ± 0.10, 9.00 ± 0.10, and 11.00 ± 0.10, respectively, using diluted HCl or NaOH (0.1 M).

Fig. 1 a shows the images of the different emission fluorescent CQDs samples under a 365 nm UV lamp. Under UV light, the CQDs displayed pronounced blue, green, red, cyan, and orange emissions. Its optical characteristics were significantly impacted by the various functional groups on the sample surface, resulting in various emission colors. FT-IR was used to characterize the prepared multicolor emission fluorescent CQDs. According to Fig. 1b, the characteristic peaks of infrared absorption for the five different types of CQDs were comparatively comparable. The data revealed peaks at 3743 cm−1, 3856 cm−1, 3452 cm−1, 3346 cm−1, 1173 cm−1, and 2750 cm−1, indicating the presence of abundant hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups (OH, NH, and CO) on the surface of the CQDs. Stretching vibrational peaks of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon structures appeared during the hydrothermal process, which is an indication that these functional groups favored the dispersion and uniform compounding with the semiconductor material. CO and COC bending vibrations were attributed to the characteristic peak at 1042 cm−1. O-CQDs, C-CQDs, and G-CQDs exhibited stretching vibrational peaks at 2360 cm−1 and 2338 cm−1, corresponding to NCO. The addition of oxygen-containing functional groups into the nitrogen-doped CQDs during the preparation process resulted in a rise in the NCO content, which caused the transition from O-CQDs to G-CQDs. A distinctive NCO ternary site was formed when the ∙OH quickly attacked the carbon atom next to the nitrogen atom [42]. Previous studies have shown that the capacity of CN groups to oxidize can be greatly enhanced by the addition of oxygen.

CQDs captured the oxygen atoms in LFC0.5 by forming CO bonds at the LFC0.5/CQDs interface. Rational Oxygen Vacancies (OVs) improved the light utilization and accelerated the separation of photogenerated carriers [43]. The stretching vibration of the CN, CO bond at 1544 cm−1 for the five different types of CQDs increased from O-CQDs to G-CQDs, suggesting that the increased oxidation on the surface of CQDs caused the redshift of the fluorescence peak position in Fig. 1. (b). The five types of CQDs were composed of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. Different fluorescence colors may be caused by nitrogen and oxygen atoms that were incorporated into the sp2 backbone during the reaction, which caused CQDs to create several defective structures. Defective structures lead to numerous defective energy levels, and when electrons from the lower conduction band return to the valence band, they produce various colors of luminescence. To investigate the chemical bond composition and functional groups of the complexes, FTIR analysis of the G-CQDs, LFC0.5, and LFC0.5/G-CQDs was performed (Fig. 1c). The LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites displayed strong, broad absorption peaks in the range of 3199 cm−1-3375 cm−1, which correspond to the OH and NH stretching vibrations. The OH− and H2O groups on the surface of LFC0.5/G-CQDs aided in the adsorption of pollutants of interest. In addition, it can interact with cavities to produce hydroxyl radicals with strong oxidizing properties, thereby facilitating the photocatalytic oxidation of LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites. When comparing the FT-IR spectra of LFC0.5/G-CQDs after doping with G-CQDs, it was found that the characteristic peaks like NCO and CO were weakened or somewhat disappeared after LFC0.5 modification, indicating that the surface functional groups of G-CQDs play an important role in the composite process with LFC0.5. In comparison to pure LFC0.5, the LFC0.5/G-CQDs composite exhibited a change in peak shape at 1500–750 cm−1, which may be attributed to the interaction force between CQDs and LFC0.5. The shift of the absorption band at 542 cm−1 in the LFC0.5/G-CQDs composite indicated the formation of FeOC bonds in the LFC0.5/G-CQDs composite.

TEM was used to examine the morphological characteristics of CQDs and the newly developed LFC0.5/G-CQDs nanocomposite (Fig. 2 ). The TEM image of the LFC0.5/G-CQDs nanocomposite clearly showed G-CQDs particles and LFC0.5, as shown in Fig. 2c. In addition, agglomeration between nanocomposite particles was observed after loading the G-CQDs, which was ascribed to the FeOC chemical bonds between the CQDs and LFC0.5. The individual particles were held together firmly by these bonds. This intimate contact between the G-CQDs and LFC0.5 was conducive to electron transfer from G-CQDs to LFC0.5 during visible-light excitation, thereby reducing the probability of recombination of photogenerated carriers and consequently enhancing its photocatalytic activity. The G-CQDs were spherical with an average diameter of 3.65 nm (Fig. S1a). On the surface of LFC0.5, uniformly distributed G-CQDs and a local lattice deletion/dislocation potentially caused by the escape of surface O atoms were observed. These are advantageous for the formation of stable heterojunctions with LFC0.5 and G-CQDs. In agreement with the XRD study, the HRTEM image displayed the crystal structure of LFC0.5 perovskites, exhibiting interlayer distances of 0.18 nm and 0.2547 nm for its (110) and (200) planes, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 2e and f, the lattice spacing of G-CQDs was 0.218 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [44]. The line scans of the corresponding areas are shown in Fig. S1c. G-CQDs were effectively loaded on LFC0.5 as evidenced by the EDS-mapping analysis, which revealed the presence and even distribution of C, O, Fe, Cu, and La. (Fig. 2g–k).

The XRD patterns of pure LFC0.5 and LFC0.5/G-CQDs nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 1. The perovskite LaFeO3(JCPDS: 75-0541) exhibited satisfactory crystallinity and high purity, as evidenced by the diffraction peaks at 22.6°, 32.2°, 39.7°, 46.2°, 57.4°, 67.4°, and 76.7°, which correlate to the (100), (110), (111), (200), (211), (220), and (310) crystal planes. In addition, a trace diffraction peak corresponding to the La2CuO4 diffraction peak (JCPDS: 81-2450) was detected at 30.94° in LFC0.5 (Fig. 1d). The diffraction peaks of the composite catalyst LFC0.5/G-CQDs, however, were identical to those of LFC0.5 after G-CQD modification. No distinctive diffraction peaks that could be attributed to G-CQDs were identified, despite the intensities of the peaks being increased. This may be because G-CQDs are highly dispersed and have low loading. The findings described in this section thus showed that the crystal structure of LFC0.5 was unaffected by the precipitation of G-CQDs onto it using the solvothermal method, but that the crystallinity was somewhat increased. Following the modification of G-CQDs, a comparison of the local diffraction peak magnification revealed that the diffraction peaks of the composite catalysts were slightly shifted in the direction of a higher angle (Fig. 1e). This change might be the result of the G-CQDs being extruded, which would indicate that the G-CQDs and LFC0.5 were successfully coupled.

The Fe2p high-resolution spectra before and after G-CQDs doping on LFC0.5 are shown in Fig. 3c. Three peaks were identified in the main Fe2p3/2 spectrum that was located at 714.2 eV, 710.5 eV, and 709.8 eV and corresponded to the Fe3+ and Fe2+. After doping G-CQDs, the ratio of surface Fe3+ increased from 52.1 % to 67.7 %, indicating that electron transfer between LFC0.5 and G-CQDs had occurred. In the high-resolution XPS of Cu2p (Fig. 3d), Cu+ and Cu2+ had binding energies of 931.5 eV and 932.6 eV, respectively. Following the modification to the G-CQDs, the positions of the Cu2p and Fe2p binding energies underwent a slight shift, with Cu2p shifting to the high field and Fe2p shifting to the low field, respectively. This trend indicated that the electron cloud density around the Cu atoms decreased and increased around the Fe atoms after the modification. The change in binding energy also indicated a chemical interaction between G-CQDs and LFC0.5, and the complexation of the two enables the efficient transmission and migration of photogenerated carriers. This infers that the modification of G-CQDs onto LFC0.5 involves not only physical adsorption but also chemical interaction. Consequently, the aforementioned analysis revealed the coexistence and close integration of G-CQDs and LFC0.5 in the composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of optical and electrochemical performance
ce:section.ce:para: EIS was used to examine the charge transfer at the surface or interface of the produced photocatalysts, and the results were presented as Nyquist plots (Fig. 5). The plots showed that the radii of semicircular arcs followed the following order: LFC0.5 > LFC0.5/C-CQDs > LFC0.5/B-CQDs > LFC0.5/R-CQDs > LFC0.5/O-CQDs > LFC0.5/G-CQDs (Fig. 5a). Furthermore, the LFC0.5/G-CQDs composite catalysts had smaller radii of the semicircular arcs, indicating that they had a lower charge transfer resistance at the surface/interface and good photogenerated carrier separation ability. This provided further evidence that the introduction of G-CQDs improved photocatalytic activity. To gain insight into the recombination of photogenerated carriers, transient photocurrent reactions of pure LFC0.5 and multicolor fluorescent LFC0.5/CQDs were investigated. The currents of all composite catalyst samples were greater than those of pure LFC0.5 samples for every time interval within 300 s (Fig. 6 b), suggesting that a quick separation of photogenerated carriers takes place during the photocatalytic process. This is a result of the unique π-conjugated structure of CQDs, which can function as an effective electron donor and acceptor to support the transfer of photogenerated electrons from the LFC0.5 surface to CQDs, inhibiting the intrinsic complexation of carriers. The transient photocurrent reaction showed the highest current density in LFC0.5/G-CQDs. The LFC0.5/G-CQDs, which have the smallest energy band gap, produced an outcome that was consistent with the EIS findings. Under the same optical excitation, the electrons of the materials with smaller band gaps were more likely to jump, displaying maximal photocurrent density and small radius impedance at the same time.

Due to the superior electron transfer properties of CQDs, the separation of the photogenerated charge carriers in the LFC0.5/CQDs composites was improved, thereby enhancing the photocatalyst's degradation performance. To enhance photocatalytic efficacy, the catalyst lifetime in the degradation reaction had to be significantly increased [48]. Thus, the PL and TRPL spectra of different LFC0.5/CQDs composites were collected. In comparison to the other four composites, LFC0.5/G-CQDs exhibited the lowest PL intensity, indicating that the high accumulation capacity of the photocarrier was difficult to combine (Fig. 5c). The time-resolved fluorescence spectra of various LFC0.5/CQDs samples are shown in Fig. 5d. By mono-exponential fitting of the data, the fluorescence lifetimes of the different LFC0.5/CQDs samples were optimized to be 0.82 ns (LFC0.5/B-CQDs), 1.52 ns (LFC0.5/G-CQDs), 0.68 ns (LFC0.5/C-CQDs), 1.22 ns (LFC0.5/O-CQDs), and 1.07 ns (LFC0.5/R-CQDs), respectively. Compared to the other photocatalysts, LFC0.5/G-CQDs had an extended emission lifetime. The extended fluorescence lifetime demonstrated that G-CQDs improved migration efficacy and suppressed the recombination of electron-hole pairs of LFC0.5. The relaxation processes of residential charge carriers were significantly different. Fluorescence and electrochemical data analysis showed that G-CQDs functioned as an effective electron acceptor and transport medium, providing LFC0.5 a better ability to separate and transfer charges when exposed to visible light. This charge separation and transfer improved the photocatalytic performance of the LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of quinoline degradation using different photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 depicts the changes in absorbance of quinoline after degradation by pure LFC0.5, multicolor fluorescent LFC0.5/CQDs composites, and LFC0.5/G-CQDs with varying amounts of CQDs when exposed to visible light for 120 min. After 120 min of exposure to visible light, the results showed that pure LFC0.5 had no discernible impact on quinoline. Due to its high persistence and difficulty in being immediately photodegraded by pure LFC0.5, the degradation rate was only 18.79 %. All the LFC0.5/CQDs composites outperformed pure LFC0.5 in photocatalytic efficiency (Fig. 6a), which can be attributed to the incorporation of CQDs that not only utilize visible light but also enhance the separation of H+ and e-. According to the findings, LFC0.5/G-CQDs, which had a 91 % efficiency in removing quinoline, were the ideal composite photocatalyst. Consistent with the findings of the optoelectronics property test, the corresponding reaction rate constant was as high as 0.014 min−1, i.e., 14 times that of pure LFC0.5 (Fig. S3a). In the presence of visible light, the aforementioned results conclusively demonstrated that LFC0.5/G-CQDs have considerable potential for quinoline removal. However, excessive CQDs had a detrimental effect on photocatalytic performance (Fig. 6b). Although CQDs functioned as active sites, excessive CQDs cover LFC0.5, resulting in light shielding and a decrease in light utilization efficiency. In addition, the excess OVs induced by an abundance of CQDs serve as the recombination center for photogenerated carriers. Thus, the photocatalytic performance of the 5 % and 10 % loaded catalysts was lower than that of the 3 % loaded catalysts. Consequently, LFC0.5/G-CQDs (3 %) were utilized in subsequent experiments.

Catalyst dosage is one of the most significant parameters influencing degradation efficiency. Fig. 6c depicts the influence of LFC0.5/G-CQDs dosage on quinoline removal. The degradation efficiency of quinoline increased gradually as the LFC0.5/G-CQDs dosage increased from 0.1 g/L to 0.5 g/L within 120 min. This was caused by an increase in active sites and free radicals as the catalyst concentration increased. However, when the catalyst dosage was increased from 0.5 g/L to more than 1.0 g/L, the degradation rate decreased significantly. Inhibition of the photocatalytic process likely occurred when adding too much catalyst, which caused the reaction solution to become more turbid and thus reduced the amount of light that reached the surface of the quinoline and reacted with it, leading to the maximum Kobs being obtained at a dosage of 0.5 g/L. Therefore, 0.5 g/L was used as the catalyst dosage for the subsequent single-factor experiments.

The pH of the catalyst can influence its adsorption mode with quinoline molecules, the state of its surface hydroxyl groups, and the production of active free radicals. Consequently, the pH can have a substantial effect on the efficacy of photocatalysis. Using LFC0.5/G-CQDs catalysts, quinoline removal studies were conducted in systems with pH values varying from 3 to 11 (Fig. 6e). The LFC0.5/G-CQDs catalysts in strong acid had extremely weak photocatalytic activity, with a rate content of only 0.002 min−1. The pKa of quinoline was 4.9, and the pHpzc of LFC0.5/G-CQDs was about 5.3 (Fig. S5). This means that for pH < 5.3, the LFC0.5/G-CQDs surface is positively charged, while for pH > 5.3, the LFC0.5/G-CQDs surface is negatively charged. Quinoline was not dissociated and LFC0.5/G-CQDs was positively charged at the initial pH of 3. This prevented the degradation of quinoline. The poor photocatalytic activity was observed for LFC0.5/G-CQDs at higher pH values. This may be because the interaction of the catalyst causes an increase in the repulsion between the negatively charged pollutant and the ionized anion. The degradation reaction was therefore inhibited. A further explanation might be the precipitation of Cu2+ and Fe2+ on the catalyst surface, which delays the cycling of the active metals. LFC0.5/G-CQDs photocatalysis exhibited exceptionally high quinoline removal rates at pH 5.0, 7.0, and 9.0, indicating that LFC0.5/G-CQDs photocatalysis has a broad range of practical applications in both weakly alkaline and acidic environments. In a prior study, the pHpzc of LFC0.5 was around 6.8, and the doping of G-CQDs caused the catalyst's zeta potential value to shift to the left. This suggests that doping of G-CQDs can weaken the electrostatic repulsive force between the catalyst and the contaminant and increase the contact area.

The effect of dissolved oxygen during the reaction on photocatalytic activity is dependent on the degradation mechanism of the pollutant. To investigate the effect of dissolved oxygen on the photocatalytic degradation of quinoline by LFC0.5/G-CQDs, the systems were oxygenated and nitrogenated separately to compare the degradation effect. It was discovered that oxygenating the system improved the efficiency and rate of quinoline degradation, whereas nitrogenating the system had the reverse effect. The rate of quinoline degradation was found to be 93.6 % when oxygen aeration was introduced to the reaction system, and the reaction rate constant was found to be 0.017 min−1. Quinoline degraded at a rate of 54.5 % when nitrogen was added to the reaction solution, and the reaction rate constant was 0.005 min−1 (Fig. 6d). This may be because there was no dissolved oxygen in the reaction solution following the passage of nitrogen gas, and the dissolved nitrogen molecules in the solution failed to effectively trap the photogenerated electrons, causing a rapid complexation of significant amounts of photogenerated e- and h+ and slowing the rate of quinoline degradation. Aeration oxygen raised the amount of dissolved oxygen in the system, and the O2 adsorbed on the catalyst surface can be reduced to ∙O2 − as an electron acceptor (Eqs. (1)), which can directly oxidize quinoline to small organic molecules, thereby accelerating the degradation of quinoline. The aeration-induced bubbles, however, also had an impact on the photocatalyst's ability to excite light, as well as the concentration of free radicals that were produced.

Fig. S6 demonstrates that the ionic leaching rate of the catalyst's active component is extremely low, proving the stability of the catalyst. Furthermore, the synthesized LFC0.5/G-CQDs showed no notable change in their quinoline degradation performance even after multiple uses (Fig. 6f), suggesting that the LFC0.5/G-CQDs can be used as efficient and stable photocatalysts for the degradation of organic compounds.

Benzoquinone (BQ), Sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4), and tert-butanol (TBA) were added as the quenchers of ∙O2 −, H+, and ∙OH, respectively (See Fig. 7 a) [49], to identify the active species produced by LFC0.5/G-CQDs in the photocatalytic degradation of quinoline. The degradation of quinoline was slightly inhibited by the addition of Na2C2O4, suggesting that the influence of H+ on photocatalytic performance is not very significant. The inclusion of BQ and TBA, however, significantly reduced the photodegradation process. BQ (17.2 %) had a greater inhibitory impact on quinoline degradation rates than TBA (29.9 %), suggesting that ∙O2 −. was the main radical in the LFC0.5/G-CQDs heterogeneous photocatalyst system. Additionally, pure LFC0.5 and LFC0.5/G-CQD composites were subjected to EPR studies to determine the presence of ∙OH and ∙O2 − under visible light irradiation. In the pure LFC0.5 photocatalytic system, there were no detectable EPR signals. In contrast, it was discovered that the signals of DMPO-∙OH and DMPO-∙O2 − for LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites were significantly stronger than those of pure LFC0.5 (Fig. 7b, c). This suggests that more ∙OH and ∙O2 − were produced after doping with the G-CQDs, which is in good agreement with the above free radical quenching experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy band structures and the possible degradation mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The energy and structure of the LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites were determined to elucidate the degradation mechanisms of the LFC0.5/G-CQDs photocatalysis processes. The VB positions of pure LFC0.5 and LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites were determined using XPS valence spectra. The VB of LFC0.5 and LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites was calculated to be 1.13 eV and 0.78 eV, respectively (Fig. 7d, e). According to the calculations made in the preceding section, the calculated Eg values for the pure LFC0.5 and LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites were 1.85 and 1.43 eV, respectively. (Fig. 4b). Consequently, the CB gaps of the pure LFC0.5 and LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites were estimated to be −0.72 and −0.65 eV, respectively, using Ec = Ev − Eg [50]. The LUMO and HOMO of G-CQDs were determined to be −0.35 eV and 2.58 eV, respectively [51]. Due to the lack of an electron mediator, photo-carrier e-/H+ could be transferred directly through the interfacial contact between LFC0.5 and G-CQDs. The photocatalyst's redox ability was improved, indicating that direct heterojunctions were most likely formed between LFC0.5 and G-CQDs. The VB position of LFC0.5 (1.13 eV) had a significantly higher negative value than the standard redox potential of ∙OH/OH− (1.99 eV). Therefore, the holes accumulated on the VB of LFC0.5 were unable to oxidize OH− to ∙OH and could only react with the small amount of water present in the system to generate a small amount of ∙OH radicals with a low signal intensity, which was challenging to detect in the EPR spectrum. However, the identification of active species showed that ∙OH was generated in the LFC0.5/G-CQDs photocatalytic system. The findings of the EPR test demonstrated that redox reactions took place at the electrons in the conduction band of the G-CQDs and the valence band holes of LFC0.5. Since the CB gaps of LFC0.5 (−0.72 eV) had a significantly higher negative value than the potentials of ∙O2 −/O2(−0.33 eV), and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of G-CQDs (2.58 eV) had a significantly higher positive value than the potentials of ∙OH/OH− (1.99 eV), both ∙OH and ∙O2 − were produced in the LFC0.5/G-CQDs photocatalytic system. Thus, a Z-type heterojunction mechanism rather than a type II heterojunction mechanism should have been used for the efficient charge transfer between LFC0.5 and G-CQDs. Based on the analysis presented above, the photocatalytic processes of LFC0.5 /G-CQDs composites were hypothesized to occur as follows: under visible light irradiation, LFC0.5 and G-CQDs were simultaneously excited, and photogenerated electrons were excited from VB to CB. After that, the electrons that were on the CB of the G-CQDs were transported to the VB of the LFC0.5 via a heterogeneous interface, where they complexed with the hole quenching that was on the VB of the LFC0.5. The photogenerated electrons on the CB of LFC0.5 possessed a potent reducing ability, converting O2 to ∙O2 − radicals. Consequently, the H+ on the valence band of LFC0.5 also possessed a potent oxidizing ability, converting H2O to ∙OH (see Scheme 1 ). The Z-type charge transfer mechanism facilitated the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes [52], which substantially increased the lifetimes of photogenerated electrons in the conduction band of LFC0.5 and holes in the valence band of G-CQDs and increased the redox capability.

Fig. 7e shows the CV curves of pure LFC0.5 and LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites. The CV curves of pure LFC0.5 and LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites were comparable. The CV curves of pure LFC0.5 and LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites possessed comparable morphologies, excellent symmetry, and distinct redox peaks. However, following doping with G-CQDs, the reduced peak potentials of the samples were shifted in a positive direction, suggesting that the reduction reaction was more likely to take place. Due to the increase in oxygen vacancies, the area of CV curve closure for LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites was significantly greater than that for pure LFC0.5. It was demonstrated that the doped G-CQDs LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites possessed photoreduction properties and an enhanced electron transfer ability, which could compensate for the absence of reduction ability in pure LFC0.5. These findings showed that doping G-CQDs in LFC0.5/G-CQD composites increased the photocatalytic effect and improved electron-hole separation.

In this investigation, the hydrothermal method was used to create five CQDs with various luminescent colors, followed by their loading on LFC0.5. The LFC0.5/G-CQDs composites displayed considerably higher photocatalytic activity towards the degradation of quinoline when exposed to visible light, as determined by photoelectron properties, structural characterizations, and performance tests. When exposed to visible light, 91 % of the quinoline was successfully photodegraded in 120 min at a rate constant of 0.01407 min−1. In comparison to the other four composites, LFC0.5/G-CQDs displayed the strongest absorption band edges, transient optical response, fluorescence lifespan, low fluorescence intensity, small radius impedance, and the narrowest band gap. According to findings from experimental quenching and EPR, the dominant radicals in the LFC0.5/G-CQDs photocatalytic process were ∙OH and ∙O2 −. The band structure analysis revealed a direct Z-scheme charge carrier transfer mechanism for the LFC0.5/G-CQDs. Thus, these findings suggested some potential uses for the design of CQDs-loaded semiconductor photocatalysts for wastewater treatment.
